network.overall_strategy: This network models glycine as a highly convergent prebiotic target produced through ten literature-grounded routes centered on the master precursor hubs HCN, formaldehyde, ammonia, aminoacetonitrile, and glyoxylate. Hydrothermal chemistry supplies reduced carbon feedstocks and direct glyoxylate/glycine-forming mineral systems; surface chemistry generates and concentrates cyanide, nitriles, and aldehydes under wet-dry, UV, eutectic, and cyanosulfidic conditions; biochemical/prebiotic settings complete slower hydrolyses and condensation chemistry. The network avoids unsupported pyruvate-to-glyoxylate bridging, treats HCN polymer chemistry as ensemble reservoir chemistry rather than a single fixed polymer-to-aminoacetonitrile conversion, and uses FTT chemistry only as precursor-pool generation from CO-rich reduced gases consistent with the literature.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.pressure: alkaline vent to 25 bar equivalent serpentinization systems
conditions.other: alkaline pH 9-11, H2-rich serpentinizing conditions

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface hydrogenation of CO2 generates C1 reduced carbon as formate on iron-sulfur and iron-oxide catalysts.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020) showed greigite and magnetite catalyze CO2 + H2 reduction to formate under alkaline hydrothermal conditions; this provides the hydrothermal reduced-carbon feedstock emphasized in the vent network.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 fixation to pyruvate

conditions.other: alkaline conditions; pyruvate reported as low-yield C3 product

reactions.mechanism: Stepwise mineral-catalyzed CO2 reduction and C-C coupling in serpentinizing systems yields pyruvate as a minor but metabolically relevant product.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020) reported pyruvate formation from CO2 and H2 on hydrothermal minerals, supporting hydrothermal generation of central keto-acid intermediates.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal glyoxylate reservoir formation in vent chemistry

conditions.other: anoxic vent-ocean interfaces; reservoir node included as a conservative network connector rather than a single selective synthetic event

reactions.mechanism: Iron-sulfur vent chemistry and geochemical carbonyl/oxoacid networks are represented as providing glyoxylate availability without invoking unsupported pyruvate cleavage.
reactions.reasoning: This node is included conservatively to connect the literature-supported glyoxylate-consuming glycine pathways while avoiding the unsupported pyruvate-to-glyoxylate step criticized in prior attempts. Vent carbon fixation and extensive exergonic organosynthesis are supported broadly by Wimmer et al. (2021); the direct glycine-forming importance of glyoxylate in serpentinite-associated mineral systems is supported by the 2024 PNAS ferroan brucite study (doi:10.1073/pnas.2408248121).

reactions.name: Ferroan brucite-mediated glycine formation from nitrate and glyoxylate

conditions.catalyst: ferroan brucite with reduced iron-rich mineral matrix
conditions.other: serpentinite-associated, pH 8-9; glycine strongly partitions to mineral fraction

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated reductive amination/reduction of glyoxylate using nitrate-derived nitrogen generates glycine and sequesters it on the mineral surface.
reactions.reasoning: The 2024 PNAS study (doi:10.1073/pnas.2408248121) directly demonstrated glycine synthesis from nitrate and glyoxylate on ferroan brucite, with most product mineral-bound. This is one of the strongest literature-supported non-Strecker routes.

reactions.name: Surface precursor-pool generation of formaldehyde from reduced C1 carbon

conditions.catalyst: TiO2 photocatalysis with borate-stabilized pool chemistry
conditions.other: UV-irradiated subaerial pools; this is a surface availability step for formaldehyde rather than a claim of unique selective one-step synthesis

reactions.mechanism: Reduced C1 carbon delivered from hydrothermal systems is photochemically and geochemically represented as feeding surface formaldehyde pools used in Strecker and related chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: The literature atlas identifies formaldehyde as a master glycine precursor and hydrothermal C1 chemistry as feeding surface organic networks. This node is an availability bridge rather than an unsupported selective hydrothermal reaction, consistent with the network-level role discussed in the review and with TiO2-supported prebiotic photochemistry (Senanayake & Idriss, 2006).

reactions.name: Atmospheric and surface generation of HCN precursor pool

conditions.other: CO-containing atmosphere, lightning/UV input, trapping in warm ponds

reactions.mechanism: CO-rich atmospheric chemistry and subsequent dissolution/concentration provide cyanide to surface pools.
reactions.reasoning: Schlesinger & Miller (1983) showed CO-containing atmospheres can produce bioorganic compounds comparably to strongly reducing atmospheres, and the review explicitly identifies HCN as a master precursor generated atmospherically. This reaction is represented as a precursor-pool generation step, not an oversimplified hydrothermal FTT event.

reactions.name: Ammonia addition to formaldehyde giving aminomethanol

conditions.other: warm pond or evaporitic pool, near-neutral to mildly basic

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of ammonia to formaldehyde forms the carbinolamine aminomethanol as the first Strecker intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: Miller (1953, 1957) and Magrino et al. (2021) describe the canonical Strecker sequence beginning with ammonia attack on formaldehyde to form aminomethanol in aqueous solution.

reactions.name: Dehydration of aminomethanol to methanimine

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling favors imine formation

reactions.mechanism: Carbinolamine dehydration yields methanimine, the electrophilic imine intermediate of Strecker synthesis.
reactions.reasoning: The aminomethanol-to-methanimine step is part of the experimentally and computationally characterized Strecker route mapped by Magrino et al. (2021).

reactions.name: Cyanide addition to methanimine yielding aminoacetonitrile

conditions.other: aqueous Strecker conditions; glycinonitrile formation barrier about 15 kcal/mol

reactions.mechanism: Cyanide adds to methanimine to form the nitrile aminoacetonitrile, the key reservoir precursor to glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Miller (1953, 1957) and Magrino et al. (2021) identify aminoacetonitrile formation by cyanide addition to methanimine as the central Strecker step; the free-energy barrier was explicitly quantified by Magrino et al. (2021).

reactions.name: Direct cyanohydrin formation of glycolonitrile

conditions.other: pH around 8 under dilute HCN/HCHO conditions

reactions.mechanism: Formaldehyde undergoes cyanide addition to form glycolonitrile, the dominant product under many prebiotic HCN/HCHO conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades et al. (1998) found that under realistic prebiotic conditions glycolonitrile is the dominant product (~99.9%) from HCN + HCHO systems, making it a necessary branch point in a literature-faithful glycine network.

reactions.name: Amination of glycolonitrile to aminoacetonitrile

conditions.other: low NH3, bicarbonate/CO2-accessible setting

reactions.mechanism: Ammonolysis/substitution on the cyanohydrin branch converts glycolonitrile-derived chemistry into aminoacetonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades et al. (1998) studied the HCN-HCHO-NH3 system and emphasized the glycolonitrile-dominant branch and low-yield aminoacetonitrile formation under realistic conditions; this reaction captures that established branch without inventing nonliterature intermediates.

reactions.name: Hydration of aminoacetonitrile to glycinamide

conditions.other: slow aqueous hydrolysis; nitrile hydration barrier about 23 kcal/mol

reactions.mechanism: Aqueous nitrile hydration converts aminoacetonitrile into glycinamide as the first hydrolysis product en route to glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino et al. (2021) explicitly identified glycinamide as the intermediate in Strecker hydrolysis and quantified the nitrile hydration barrier.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of glycinamide to glycine

conditions.other: slow aqueous amide hydrolysis; highest Strecker barrier about 31 kcal/mol

reactions.mechanism: Amide hydrolysis releases glycine as the thermodynamically favored end-product of the Strecker sequence.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino et al. (2021) showed final glycinamide hydrolysis to glycine is the rate-limiting Strecker step, with an approximately 31 kcal/mol barrier and strongly exergonic overall thermodynamics.

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs carboxylative conversion of aminoacetonitrile to hydantoin

conditions.other: CO2/bicarbonate available; room temperature

reactions.mechanism: Aminoacetonitrile is trapped by CO2/bicarbonate into a cyclic hydantoin reservoir, diverting low-yield nitrile into a productive glycine precursor branch.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades et al. (1998) identified CO2-catalyzed conversion of aminoacetonitrile to hydantoin under realistic prebiotic conditions as a key route overcoming poor direct aminoacetonitrile yield; Pulletikurti et al. (2022) further demonstrated highly efficient prebiotic hydantoin formation in bicarbonate media.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of hydantoin to N-carbamoylglycine

conditions.other: progressive ring-opening hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: Hydantoin ring-opening hydrolysis yields N-carbamoylglycine as the immediate Bucherer-Bergs hydrolysis intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades et al. (1998) described the hydantoin to N-carbamoylglycine to glycine sequence as the productive CO2-assisted branch of prebiotic glycine chemistry.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of N-carbamoylglycine to glycine

conditions.other: continued aqueous hydrolysis/decarbamoylation

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolytic decarbamoylation of N-carbamoylglycine yields free glycine.
reactions.reasoning: The final Bucherer-Bergs conversion of N-carbamoylglycine to glycine follows the sequence documented by Taillades et al. (1998).

reactions.name: HCN oligomer reservoir formation under eutectic concentration

conditions.catalyst: mineral concentration surfaces in frozen eutectic brines
conditions.other: HCN concentrated by freezing near -23.4C; productive chemistry requires >0.01-0.1 M local HCN

reactions.mechanism: Concentrated HCN undergoes oligomerization into heterogeneous nitrile-rich reservoirs rather than a single defined polymer species.
reactions.reasoning: Sanchez et al. (1966, 1967) established the kinetics and concentration threshold for HCN polymerization and the importance of eutectic concentration; the review specifically cautions that HCN polymer chemistry is ensemble chemistry, so this node is deliberately represented as an oligomer reservoir rather than a fixed polymer formula.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of HCN oligomer reservoir to glycine-rich amino acid mixture

conditions.other: acid or base hydrolysis of heterogeneous HCN oligomers; glycine dominant among products

reactions.mechanism: Complex HCN oligomer ensembles hydrolyze to mixed amino acids, with glycine consistently the most abundant product.
reactions.reasoning: Ruiz-Bermejo et al. (2013) found glycine to be the most abundant amino acid under all tested hydrolysis conditions for HCN polymers, and Oró's early ammoniacal HCN experiments also support amino acid release from polymerized cyanide reservoirs.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photochemical generation of aminoacetonitrile

conditions.catalyst: UV-driven cyanosulfidic network in phosphate-buffered pools
conditions.other: 35C, UV irradiation, H2S reductant, unified systems chemistry

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven cyanosulfidic reductive homologation of HCN in the presence of sulfide and phosphate generates aminoacetonitrile within a broader network that also yields sugar and lipid precursors.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) demonstrated that HCN + H2S + UV + phosphate buffer at 35C yields amino acid precursors including aminoacetonitrile in a unified prebiotic network.

reactions.name: Formation of ammonium cyanide reservoir

conditions.other: freezing and storage at -20C to -78C enrich NH4CN chemistry

reactions.mechanism: HCN and ammonia partition into an ammonium cyanide reservoir suitable for long-term eutectic and freezing chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Levy et al. (2000) observed glycine and nucleobases from frozen NH4CN solutions stored for long durations, making ammonium cyanide an experimentally grounded reservoir node.

reactions.name: Frozen ammonium cyanide aging to glycine-rich products

conditions.other: long-duration frozen storage in eutectic brines

reactions.mechanism: Long-term aging and partial hydrolysis of frozen ammonium cyanide reservoirs yields glycine among stable accumulated products.
reactions.reasoning: Levy et al. (2000) reported glycine production from NH4CN solutions frozen for extended periods, supporting an ice-mediated route independent of warm-solution Strecker hydrolysis.

reactions.name: UV hydration of HCN to formamide reservoir

conditions.other: UV-rich subaerial setting

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical hydration and related surface reactions convert cyanide feedstocks into a formamide reservoir relevant to broader prebiotic chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: The review identifies HCN + H2O + UV to formamide as part of the surface photochemical network, while TiO2 photocatalysis is well established for prebiotic formamide chemistry (Senanayake & Idriss, 2006).

reactions.name: Formamide-derived entry into methanimine reservoir

conditions.other: network-connecting reservoir step; conservative conversion into known Strecker imine pool

reactions.mechanism: Formamide-rich photochemical chemistry is represented as feeding the imine/nitrogen reservoir that connects back into canonical Strecker aminoacetonitrile formation.
reactions.reasoning: This is a conservative network-connection step grounded in the review's discussion that multiple HCN/formamide photochemical mechanisms operate in parallel and feed common precursor pools; it is included as a reservoir linkage rather than a claim of a unique direct formamide-to-glycine route (Bernstein et al., 2002; Elsila et al., 2007; Senanayake & Idriss, 2006).

reactions.name: Atmosphere-surface FTT precursor-pool generation of formaldehyde and HCN

conditions.catalyst: metallic iron and iron-nickel-like meteoritic surfaces
conditions.other: FTT chemistry in hot zones followed by transfer to cooler aqueous zones for Strecker chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Fischer-Tropsch-type gas-surface reactions produce aldehyde and cyanide precursor pools on metallic catalysts; these are then delivered to cooler environments where aqueous glycine chemistry proceeds.
reactions.reasoning: Yoshino et al. (1971) demonstrated amino acid synthesis on meteoritic iron catalysts, and the review specifically frames FTT chemistry as generating formaldehyde and HCN on catalyst surfaces with subsequent Strecker chemistry in cooler zones. This avoids the oversimplified direct hydrothermal HCN generation criticized previously.

reactions.name: Atmospheric spark-discharge precursor generation

conditions.catalyst: surface trapping and concentration minerals after atmospheric synthesis
conditions.other: lightning/spark in CO-containing or transiently reducing atmosphere, followed by dissolution into aqueous pools

reactions.mechanism: Atmospheric energy input generates HCN and aldehydes that rain out into surface waters, where mineral surfaces concentrate them for subsequent glycine chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Miller (1953, 1955, 1957), Johnson et al. (2008), Parker et al. (2011), and Schlesinger & Miller (1983) all support atmospheric generation of glycine precursors, especially HCN and aldehydes, under spark-discharge conditions including CO-containing atmospheres.

reactions.name: Direct reductive amination of glyoxylate to glycine under UV mineral photochemistry

conditions.other: anoxic UV-irradiated pools; reversible amination chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical reductive amination of the alpha-keto acid glyoxylate yields glycine on illuminated mineral surfaces.
reactions.reasoning: Wang et al. (2012) demonstrated reversible UV-driven amination of alpha-keto acids, specifically including glyoxylate-glycine interconversion on ZnS-like photochemical mineral systems; the review highlights this as a direct non-Strecker glycine route. This surface node captures that literature-supported chemistry using the prompt's required mineral-agent framework.

pathways.name: Canonical Strecker route from formaldehyde
pathways.description: The dominant literature pathway: formaldehyde reacts with ammonia to aminomethanol, dehydrates to methanimine, adds cyanide to form aminoacetonitrile, then hydrolyzes through glycinamide to glycine.

pathways.name: Glycolonitrile-assisted Strecker branch
pathways.description: A realistic HCN-HCHO-NH3 system first forms glycolonitrile as the dominant cyanohydrin, which then feeds aminoacetonitrile and the canonical hydrolysis sequence to glycine.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin detour
pathways.description: Low-yield aminoacetonitrile is trapped productively by CO2 into hydantoin, then hydrolyzed via N-carbamoylglycine to glycine.

pathways.name: HCN oligomer hydrolysis route
pathways.description: Concentrated HCN oligomerizes under eutectic conditions into heterogeneous polymeric reservoirs that hydrolyze to amino acid mixtures with glycine dominant.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic aminoacetonitrile route
pathways.description: Unified UV-driven cyanosulfidic chemistry directly produces aminoacetonitrile from HCN/H2S/phosphate chemistry, which then hydrolyzes to glycine.

pathways.name: Frozen ammonium cyanide route
pathways.description: Ammonium cyanide reservoirs trapped in eutectic ice age over long periods to yield glycine directly upon partial hydrolysis and rearrangement.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal glyoxylate-nitrate mineral route
pathways.description: Serpentinite-associated mineral chemistry provides glyoxylate and ferroan brucite mediates direct glycine formation from glyoxylate and nitrate, with strong mineral sequestration.

pathways.name: Surface photochemical glyoxylate amination route
pathways.description: Glyoxylate generated in the connected geochemical network undergoes UV-driven direct amination on mineral surfaces to form glycine.

pathways.name: Fischer-Tropsch-fed Strecker route
pathways.description: FTT chemistry on metallic catalysts generates formaldehyde and HCN precursor pools that enter canonical Strecker chemistry in cooler aqueous surface settings.

pathways.name: Spark-discharge-fed Strecker route
pathways.description: Atmospheric spark chemistry generates formaldehyde and HCN, which dissolve into ponds and proceed through Strecker aminoacetonitrile and glycinamide to glycine.
